                else
                    value = μ - σ * log(-log(rand()))
                end
                push!(site_values, value)
            end
            
            # Regional maximum (weighted)
            regional_max = sum(region_weights .* site_values)
            push!(regional_maxima, regional_max)
        end
        
        # Estimate T-year return level
        return_level = quantile(regional_maxima, 1 - 1/T)
        regional_return_levels[T] = return_level
    end
    
    return regional_return_levels, site_params
end

# Example: Estimate return levels for different return periods
annual_maxima = zeros(50, n_sites)  # 50 years of annual maxima

# Simulate annual maxima (simplified)
for year in 1:50
    for site in 1:n_sites
        # Use block maxima from daily precipitation
        year_start = (year-1) * 20 + 1
        year_end = min(year * 20, size(precip_data, 1))
        annual_maxima[year, site] = maximum(precip_data[year_start:year_end, site])
    end
end

return_periods = [10, 25, 50, 100]
regional_rl, site_fits = estimate_regional_return_levels(annual_maxima, locations, return_periods)

println("Regional Return Levels:")
for (T, rl) in regional_rl
    println("$T-year return level: $(round(rl, digits=2))")
end

2.10.2 7.6.2 Uncertainty Quantification

Bootstrap methods provide a natural approach for quantifying uncertainty in spatial extreme value estimates.

"""
    bootstrap_return_levels(data, locations, return_periods, n_bootstrap=1000)

Bootstrap confidence intervals for spatial return levels.
"""
function bootstrap_return_levels(data, locations, return_periods; n_bootstrap=1000)
    n_years = size(data, 1)
    
    bootstrap_estimates = []
    
    for b in 1:n_bootstrap
        # Bootstrap sample years
        boot_indices = sample(1:n_years, n_years, replace=true)
        boot_data = data[boot_indices, :]
        
        # Estimate return levels for bootstrap sample
        boot_rl, _ = estimate_regional_return_levels(boot_data, locations, return_periods)
        push!(bootstrap_estimates, boot_rl)
    end
    
    # Compute confidence intervals
    confidence_intervals = Dict()
    for T in return_periods
        boot_values = [est[T] for est in bootstrap_estimates]
        ci_lower = quantile(boot_values, 0.025)
        ci_upper = quantile(boot_values, 0.975)
        confidence_intervals[T] = (ci_lower, ci_upper)
    end
    
    return confidence_intervals
end

# Compute bootstrap confidence intervals
ci_results = bootstrap_return_levels(annual_maxima, locations, return_periods, n_bootstrap=200)

println("\n95% Confidence Intervals for Regional Return Levels:")
for T in return_periods
    lower, upper = ci_results[T]
    point_est = regional_rl[T]
    println("$T-year: $(round(point_est, digits=2)) [$(round(lower, digits=2)), $(round(upper, digits=2))]")
end

2.11 7.7 Applications to Environmental Extremes

2.11.1 7.7.1 Extreme Precipitation Analysis

Extreme precipitation events require careful modeling of both the intensity and spatial extent of rainfall patterns. The approach combines POT methods with spatial dependence modeling.

"""
    analyze_precipitation_extremes(precip_data, locations, coords_time, threshold_quantile=0.99)

Comprehensive analysis of extreme precipitation events including spatial clustering.
"""
function analyze_precipitation_extremes(precip_data, locations, coords_time; threshold_quantile=0.99)
    n_days, n_sites = size(precip_data)
    
    # Step 1: Identify extreme events
    site_thresholds = [quantile(precip_data[:, i], threshold_quantile) for i in 1:n_sites]
    
    # Step 2: Define spatial extreme events
    extreme_events = []
    
    for day in 1:n_days
        daily_precip = precip_data[day, :]
        exceeding_sites = findall(daily_precip .> site_thresholds)
        
        if length(exceeding_sites) >= 3  # Minimum spatial extent
            event = (
                day = day,
                sites = exceeding_sites,
                values = daily_precip[exceeding_sites],
                spatial_extent = length(exceeding_sites),
                total_volume = sum(daily_precip[exceeding_sites])
            )
            push!(extreme_events, event)
        end
    end
    
    # Step 3: Characterize spatial patterns
    spatial_extents = [evt.spatial_extent for evt in extreme_events]
    total_volumes = [evt.total_volume for evt in extreme_events]
    
    # Step 4: Temporal clustering analysis
    event_days = [evt.day for evt in extreme_events]
    inter_arrival_times = diff(event_days)
    
    results = (
        events = extreme_events,
        n_events = length(extreme_events),
        mean_spatial_extent = mean(spatial_extents),
        mean_volume = mean(total_volumes),
        mean_inter_arrival = mean(inter_arrival_times),
        thresholds = site_thresholds
    )
    
    return results
end

# Apply to simulated precipitation data
extreme_analysis = analyze_precipitation_extremes(precip_data, locations, 1:size(precip_data,1))

println("Extreme Precipitation Analysis Results:")
println("Number of extreme events: $(extreme_analysis.n_events)")
println("Mean spatial extent: $(round(extreme_analysis.mean_spatial_extent, digits=1)) sites")
println("Mean total volume: $(round(extreme_analysis.mean_volume, digits=2)) units")
println("Mean inter-arrival time: $(round(extreme_analysis.mean_inter_arrival, digits=1)) days")

2.11.2 7.7.2 Heat Wave Modeling

Heat waves represent a classic example of spatio-temporal extreme events that require modeling of both intensity and persistence.

"""
    identify_heat_waves(temperature_data, locations
\\newpage

# Chapter 8: Machine Learning for Spatio-Temporal Data

The intersection of machine learning and spatio-temporal modeling represents one of the most rapidly evolving areas in computational statistics. While traditional approaches rely on parametric models with explicit covariance structures, machine learning methods can automatically learn complex patterns from data. This chapter explores how deep learning architectures can be adapted to respect the inherent structure of space-time data, providing both predictive power and uncertainty quantification.

## 8.1 Spatio-Temporal Neural Network Architectures

### 8.1.1 Convolutional Neural Networks for Gridded Data

For regularly gridded spatio-temporal data, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) provide a natural framework that respects spatial locality and translation invariance. The key insight is that spatial patterns often repeat across different locations, making convolution an ideal operation for capturing these regularities.

> **Definition 8.1 (Spatio-Temporal Convolution).** Let f: ℝᵈ × ℝ → ℝ represent a spatio-temporal field on d-dimensional space. A spatio-temporal convolution with kernel K: ℝᵈ × ℝ → ℝ is defined as:
> 
> (K ∗ f)(s, t) = ∫∫ K(s - s', t - t') f(s', t') ds' dt'

For discrete gridded data, this becomes a discrete convolution operation that can be efficiently implemented using standard CNN architectures.

The challenge in spatio-temporal CNNs lies in handling the temporal dimension appropriately. Several architectural patterns have emerged:

**Separable Spatio-Temporal Convolutions**: These decompose the spatio-temporal convolution into separate spatial and temporal operations, reducing computational complexity while maintaining expressiveness.

**3D Convolutions**: These treat time as an additional spatial dimension, applying convolutions across space-time volumes. This approach naturally captures spatio-temporal interactions but requires careful consideration of the different scales in space and time.

```julia
using Flux, CUDA

# Define a 3D CNN for spatio-temporal data
function create_spatiotemporal_cnn(input_size, num_filters)
    return Chain(
        # First 3D convolutional layer
        Conv((3, 3, 3), 1 => num_filters, relu; pad=1),
        BatchNorm(num_filters),
        
        # Second layer with stride to downsample
        Conv((3, 3, 2), num_filters => 2*num_filters, relu; 
             pad=(1, 1, 0), stride=(1, 1, 2)),
        BatchNorm(2*num_filters),
        
        # Third layer
        Conv((3, 3, 3), 2*num_filters => 4*num_filters, relu; pad=1),
        BatchNorm(4*num_filters),
        
        # Global pooling and dense layers
        AdaptiveMaxPool((1, 1, 1)),
        Flux.flatten,
        Dense(4*num_filters => 128, relu),
        Dropout(0.5),
        Dense(128 => 1)  # Single output for regression
    )
end

# Example usage for climate data prediction
model = create_spatiotemporal_cnn((64, 64, 10), 32)

# Simulate some spatio-temporal data: 64x64 spatial grid, 10 time steps
X = randn(Float32, 64, 64, 10, 32)  # Batch size 32
y = model(X)
println("Output shape: $(size(y))")  # Should be (1, 32)

2.11.3 8.1.2 Recurrent Architectures for Sequential Modeling

While CNNs excel at capturing local spatio-temporal patterns, recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and their variants like LSTMs and GRUs are better suited for modeling long-range temporal dependencies. For spatio-temporal data, we can combine spatial feature extraction with temporal sequence modeling.

Definition 8.2 (ConvLSTM Cell). A ConvLSTM cell replaces the matrix multiplications in a standard LSTM with convolution operations:

fₜ = σ(Wₓf ∗ Xₜ + Whf ∗ Hₜ₋₁ + bf) iₜ = σ(Wₓᵢ ∗ Xₜ + Whᵢ ∗ Hₜ₋₁ + bᵢ) C̃ₜ = tanh(WₓC ∗ Xₜ + WhC ∗ Hₜ₋₁ + bC) Cₜ = fₜ ⊙ Cₜ₋₁ + iₜ ⊙ C̃ₜ oₜ = σ(Wₓₒ ∗ Xₜ + Whₒ ∗ Hₜ₋₁ + bₒ) Hₜ = oₜ ⊙ tanh(Cₒ)

where ∗ denotes convolution, ⊙ denotes element-wise multiplication, and σ is the sigmoid function.

using Flux

# Custom ConvLSTM implementation
mutable struct ConvLSTM
    Wxi::Conv
    Wxf::Conv  
    Wxo::Conv
    Wxg::Conv
    Whi::Conv
    Whf::Conv
    Who::Conv
    Whg::Conv
    state::Tuple
end

function ConvLSTM(in_channels::Int, hidden_channels::Int, kernel_size::Int)
    # Input convolutions
    Wxi = Conv((kernel_size, kernel_size), in_channels => hidden_channels, 
               bias=true, pad=kernel_size÷2)
    Wxf = Conv((kernel_size, kernel_size), in_channels => hidden_channels, 
               bias=true, pad=kernel_size÷2)
    Wxo = Conv((kernel_size, kernel_size), in_channels => hidden_channels, 
               bias=true, pad=kernel_size÷2)
    Wxg = Conv((kernel_size, kernel_size), in_channels => hidden_channels, 
               bias=true, pad=kernel_size÷2)
    
    # Hidden state convolutions
    Whi = Conv((kernel_size, kernel_size), hidden_channels => hidden_channels, 
               bias=false, pad=kernel_size÷2)
    Whf = Conv((kernel_size, kernel_size), hidden_channels => hidden_channels, 
               bias=false, pad=kernel_size÷2)
    Who = Conv((kernel_size, kernel_size), hidden_channels => hidden_channels, 
               bias=false, pad=kernel_size÷2)
    Whg = Conv((kernel_size, kernel_size), hidden_channels => hidden_channels, 
               bias=false, pad=kernel_size÷2)
    
    return ConvLSTM(Wxi, Wxf, Wxo, Wxg, Whi, Whf, Who, Whg, (nothing, nothing))
end

function (m::ConvLSTM)(x)
    h, c = m.state
    if h === nothing
        # Initialize hidden states
        h = zeros(Float32, size(x)[1:end-1]..., size(m.Wxi.weight, 4), size(x)[end])
        c = zeros(Float32, size(h))
    end
    
    # ConvLSTM operations
    i = σ.(m.Wxi(x) .+ m.Whi(h))
    f = σ.(m.Wxf(x) .+ m.Whf(h))
    o = σ.(m.Wxo(x) .+ m.Who(h))
    g = tanh.(m.Wxg(x) .+ m.Whg(h))
    
    c_new = f .* c .+ i .* g
    h_new = o .* tanh.(c_new)
    
    m.state = (h_new, c_new)
    return h_new
end

Flux.@functor ConvLSTM

# Example: Weather forecasting model
function create_weather_forecasting_model()
    return Chain(
        ConvLSTM(3, 64, 3),   # 3 input channels (temperature, humidity, pressure)
        ConvLSTM(64, 32, 3),
        Conv((1, 1), 32 => 1, sigmoid)  # Single output channel
    )
end

2.12 8.2 Graph Neural Networks for Irregular Spatial Data

Many spatio-temporal datasets are defined on irregular spatial domains or networks rather than regular grids. Graph neural networks (GNNs) provide a flexible framework for handling such data by representing spatial relationships through graph connectivity.

2.12.1 8.2.1 Spatial Graph Convolutions

Definition 8.3 (Graph Convolution). Given a graph G = (V, E) with adjacency matrix A and node features X ∈ ℝⁿˣᵈ, a graph convolution layer computes:

H^(l+1) = σ(D̃⁻¹/²ÃD̃⁻¹/²H(l)W(l))

where Ã = A + I is the adjacency matrix with added self-connections, D̃ᵢᵢ = Σⱼ Ãᵢⱼ, and W^(l) are learnable parameters.

For spatio-temporal data on graphs, we need to handle temporal dynamics while respecting the spatial graph structure. One approach is to use separate parameters for spatial and temporal relationships.

using Flux, SparseArrays, LinearAlgebra

# Graph convolution layer
struct GraphConv
    weight::AbstractMatrix
    bias::AbstractVector
    σ
end

function GraphConv(in_features::Int, out_features::Int, σ=identity)
    weight = randn(Float32, out_features, in_features) * sqrt(2.0f0 / in_features)
    bias = zeros(Float32, out_features)
    return GraphConv(weight, bias, σ)
end

Flux.@functor GraphConv

function (gc::GraphConv)(A_norm, X)
    # A_norm: normalized adjacency matrix
    # X: node features (n_nodes × n_features)
    return gc.σ.(A_norm * X * gc.weight' .+ gc.bias')
end

# Normalize adjacency matrix
function normalize_adjacency(A)
    A_tilde = A + I
    D_inv_sqrt = Diagonal(1.0 ./ sqrt.(sum(A_tilde, dims=2)[:]))
    return D_inv_sqrt * A_tilde * D_inv_sqrt
end

# Spatio-temporal GNN model
function create_spatiotemporal_gnn(n_features, hidden_dim, n_timesteps)
    return Chain(
        # Spatial feature extraction
        x -> x,  # Identity for first layer
        # We'll apply GNN operations in the forward pass
    )
end

# Example: Traffic prediction on road network
function traffic_prediction_example()
    # Simulate road network (10 nodes, sparse connectivity)
    n_nodes = 10
    A = sprandn(n_nodes, n_nodes, 0.3)  # 30% connectivity
    A = A + A'  # Make symmetric
    A = A .> 0  # Binary adjacency
    A_norm = normalize_adjacency(Matrix(A))
    
    # Simulate traffic data: 10 nodes, 5 features, 20 timesteps
    n_features = 5
    n_timesteps = 20
    traffic_data = randn(Float32, n_nodes, n_features, n_timesteps)
    
    # Graph convolution layers
    gc1 = GraphConv(n_features, 32, relu)
    gc2 = GraphConv(32, 16, relu)
    gc3 = GraphConv(16, 1)  # Predict single value (e.g., traffic flow)
    
    # Process each timestep
    results = []
    for t in 1:n_timesteps
        X_t = traffic_data[:, :, t]  # Features at time t
        
        # Apply graph convolutions
        h1 = gc1(A_norm, X_t)
        h2 = gc2(A_norm, h1)
        output = gc3(A_norm, h2)
        
        push!(results, output)
    end
    
    return hcat(results...)  # n_nodes × n_timesteps
end

predictions = traffic_prediction_example()
println("Traffic predictions shape: $(size(predictions))")

2.12.2 8.2.2 Spatio-Temporal Graph Convolutions

To handle temporal dependencies in graph-structured data, we can extend graph convolutions to incorporate temporal information. One effective approach is the spatio-temporal graph convolutional network (STGCN).

Definition 8.4 (Spatio-Temporal Graph Block). An STGCN block consists of: 1. Temporal gated convolution: captures temporal patterns 2. Spatial graph convolution: captures spatial dependencies
3. Another temporal gated convolution: refines temporal features

The output at layer l is: H^(l+1) = Γ₂ ∗ σ(Θ ∗ (Γ₁ ∗ H^(l)))

where Γ₁, Γ₂ are temporal convolutions, Θ is spatial graph convolution, and ∗ denotes convolution.

# Temporal gated convolution
struct TemporalGatedConv
    conv::Conv
    gate::Conv
end

function TemporalGatedConv(in_channels, out_channels, kernel_size)
    conv = Conv((kernel_size,), in_channels => out_channels)
    gate = Conv((kernel_size,), in_channels => out_channels, sigmoid)
    return TemporalGatedConv(conv, gate)
end

Flux.@functor TemporalGatedConv

function (tgc::TemporalGatedConv)(x)
    # x shape: (time_steps, features, nodes, batch)
    return tgc.conv(x) .* tgc.gate(x)
end

# STGCN Block
struct STGCNBlock
    temporal1::TemporalGatedConv
    spatial::GraphConv
    temporal2::TemporalGatedConv
    A_norm::Matrix{Float32}
end

function STGCNBlock(in_channels, out_channels, temporal_kernel, A_norm)
    temporal1 = TemporalGatedConv(in_channels, out_channels, temporal_kernel)
    spatial = GraphConv(out_channels, out_channels, relu)
    temporal2 = TemporalGatedConv(out_channels, out_channels, temporal_kernel)
    
    return STGCNBlock(temporal1, spatial, temporal2, A_norm)
end

Flux.@functor STGCNBlock (temporal1, spatial, temporal2)

function (block::STGCNBlock)(x)
    # x shape: (time_steps, features, nodes, batch)
    
    # First temporal convolution
    x1 = block.temporal1(x)
    
    # Spatial graph convolution (apply to each time step)
    time_steps, features, nodes, batch_size = size(x1)
    x2 = similar(x1)
    
    for t in 1:time_steps, b in 1:batch_size
        # Extract features for time t, batch b: (nodes, features)
        x_t = permutedims(x1[t, :, :, b], (2, 1))
        # Apply spatial convolution: (nodes, features) -> (nodes, features) 
        x2[t, :, :, b] = permutedims(block.spatial(block.A_norm, x_t), (2, 1))
    end
    
    # Second temporal convolution
    return block.temporal2(x2)
end

2.13 8.3 Transformer Architectures for Spatio-Temporal Data

Transformers have revolutionized natural language processing and are increasingly being applied to spatio-temporal modeling. The self-attention mechanism allows the model to capture long-range dependencies in both space and time.

2.13.1 8.3.1 Spatio-Temporal Attention

Definition 8.5 (Spatio-Temporal Self-Attention). Given input sequences representing spatio-temporal data, self-attention computes:

Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V

where Q, K, V are query, key, and value matrices derived from the input through learned linear transformations.

For spatio-temporal data, we need to incorporate both spatial and temporal positional encodings to help the model understand the structure of the data.

using Flux, LinearAlgebra

# Positional encoding for spatio-temporal data
function spatiotemporal_positional_encoding(seq_len, spatial_dim, d_model)
    # Temporal positional encoding
    pos_temp = zeros(Float32, seq_len, d_model ÷ 2)
    for pos in 1:seq_len
        for i in 1:2:d_model÷2
            pos_temp[pos, i] = sin(pos / 10000^((i-1)/d_model*2))
            if i+1 <= d_model÷2
                pos_temp[pos, i+1] = cos(pos / 10000^((i-1)/d_model*2))
            end
        end
    end
    
    # Spatial positional encoding  
    pos_spat = zeros(Float32, spatial_dim, d_model ÷ 2)
    for pos in 1:spatial_dim
        for i in 1:2:d_model÷2
            pos_spat[pos, i] = sin(pos / 10000^((i-1)/d_model*2))
            if i+1 <= d_model÷2
                pos_spat[pos, i+1] = cos(pos / 10000^((i-1)/d_model*2))
            end
        end
    end
    
    return pos_temp, pos_spat
end

# Multi-head attention for spatio-temporal data
struct SpatioTemporalAttention
    num_heads::Int
    d_model::Int
    d_k::Int
    W_q::Dense
    W_k::Dense  
    W_v::Dense
    W_o::Dense
    dropout::Dropout
end

function SpatioTemporalAttention(d_model, num_heads, dropout_rate=0.1)
    @assert d_model % num_heads == 0
    d_k = d_model ÷ num_heads
    
    W_q = Dense(d_model => d_model, bias=false)
    W_k = Dense(d_model => d_model, bias=false) 
    W_v = Dense(d_model => d_model, bias=false)
    W_o = Dense(d_model => d_model)
    dropout = Dropout(dropout_rate)
    
    return SpatioTemporalAttention(num_heads, d_model, d_k, W_q, W_k, W_v, W_o, dropout)
end

Flux.@functor SpatioTemporalAttention

function (attn::SpatioTemporalAttention)(x, mask=nothing)
    batch_size, seq_len, d_model = size(x)
    
    # Generate Q, K, V
    Q = reshape(attn.W_q(x), batch_size, seq_len, attn.num_heads, attn.d_k)
    K = reshape(attn.W_k(x), batch_size, seq_len, attn.num_heads, attn.d_k)
    V = reshape(attn.W_v(x), batch_size, seq_len, attn.num_heads, attn.d_k)
    
    # Transpose for attention computation
    Q = permutedims(Q, (1, 3, 2, 4))  # (batch, heads, seq_len, d_k)
    K = permutedims(K, (1, 3, 2, 4))
    V = permutedims(V, (1, 3, 2, 4))
    
    # Scaled dot-product attention
    scores = batched_mul(Q, permutedims(K, (1, 2, 4, 3))) / sqrt(attn.d_k)
    
    if mask !== nothing
        scores = scores .+ mask
    end
    
    attention_weights = softmax(scores, dims=4)
    attention_weights = attn.dropout(attention_weights)
    
    # Apply attention to values
    output = batched_mul(attention_weights, V)  # (batch, heads, seq_len, d_k)
    
    # Concatenate heads
    output = permutedims(output, (1, 3, 2, 4))  # (batch, seq_len, heads, d_k)
    output = reshape(output, batch_size, seq_len, attn.d_model)
    
    return attn.W_o(output)
end

# Helper function for batched matrix multiplication
function batched_mul(A, B)
    return reshape(
        reshape(A, size(A, 1) * size(A, 2), size(A, 3), size(A, 4)) * 
        reshape(B, size(B, 1) * size(B, 2), size(B, 3), size(B, 4)),
        size(A, 1), size(A, 2), size(A, 3), size(B, 4)
    )
end

2.13.2 8.3.2 Spatio-Temporal Transformer Model

Building on the attention mechanism, we can construct a complete transformer architecture adapted for spatio-temporal data:

# Transformer block for spatio-temporal data
struct SpatioTemporalTransformerBlock
    self_attention::SpatioTemporalAttention
    layer_norm1::LayerNorm
    ffn::Chain
    layer_norm2::LayerNorm
    dropout::Dropout
end

function SpatioTemporalTransformerBlock(d_model, num_heads, d_ff, dropout_rate=0.1)
    self_attention = SpatioTemporalAttention(d_model, num_heads, dropout_rate)
    layer_norm1 = LayerNorm(d_model)
    
    # Feed-forward network
    ffn = Chain(
        Dense(d_model => d_ff, relu),
        Dropout(dropout_rate),
        Dense(d_ff => d_model)
    )
    
    layer_norm2 = LayerNorm(d_model)
    dropout = Dropout(dropout_rate)
    
    return SpatioTemporalTransformerBlock(
        self_attention, layer_norm1, ffn, layer_norm2, dropout
    )
end

Flux.@functor SpatioTemporalTransformerBlock

function (block::SpatioTemporalTransformerBlock)(x, mask=nothing)
    # Self-attention with residual connection
    attn_output = block.self_attention(x, mask)
    x1 = block.layer_norm1(x + block.dropout(attn_output))
    
    # Feed-forward with residual connection  
    ffn_output = block.ffn(x1)
    x2 = block.layer_norm2(x1 + block.dropout(ffn_output))
    
    return x2
end

# Complete spatio-temporal transformer
function create_spatiotemporal_transformer(input_dim, d_model, num_heads, num_layers, d_ff)
    return Chain(
        # Input embedding
        Dense(input_dim => d_model),
        
        # Transformer blocks
        [SpatioTemporalTransformerBlock(d_model, num_heads, d_ff) 
         for _ in 1:num_layers]...,
        
        # Output layer
        Dense(d_model => 1)  # Single output for regression
    )
end

# Example usage: Climate modeling
function climate_transformer_example()
    # Model parameters
    input_dim = 4  # Temperature, humidity, pressure, wind speed
    d_model = 128
    num_heads = 8
    num_layers = 6
    d_ff = 512
    
    model = create_spatiotemporal_transformer(input_dim, d_model, num_heads, num_layers, d_ff)
    
    # Simulate climate data: 32 locations, 100 time steps, 4 variables
    batch_size = 16
    seq_len = 100
    data = randn(Float32, batch_size, seq_len, input_dim)
    
    # Add positional encoding
    pos_temp, pos_spat = spatiotemporal_positional_encoding(seq_len, 32, d_model)
    
    # Forward pass
    output = model(data)
    
    println("Climate transformer output shape: $(size(output))")
    return output
end

climate_output = climate_transformer_example()

2.14 8.4 Deep Gaussian Processes for Space-Time

Deep Gaussian processes (DGPs) extend standard Gaussian processes by composing multiple GP layers, allowing for more flexible modeling of complex spatio-temporal phenomena while maintaining principled uncertainty quantification.

2.14.1 8.4.1 Hierarchical Gaussian Process Models

Definition 8.6 (Deep Gaussian Process). A deep GP with L layers is defined recursively:

f₁(x) ~ GP(0, k₁(x, x’)) f₂(x) ~ GP(0, k₂(f₁(x), f₁(x’)))
⋮ fₗ(x) ~ GP(0, kₗ(fₗ₋₁(x), fₗ₋₁(x’)))

where each layer l has its own kernel function kₗ.

For spatio-temporal data, each layer can capture different aspects of the spatio-temporal dependencies, from local correlations in early layers to global patterns in deeper layers.

using Flux, Distributions, LinearAlgebra

# Deep Gaussian Process layer
struct DeepGPLayer
    num_inducing::Int
    input_dim::Int
    output_dim::Int
    inducing_points::Matrix{Float32}
    kernel_lengthscales::Vector{Float32}
    kernel_variance::Float32
    noise_variance::Float32
    mean_function::Dense
end

function DeepGPLayer(num_inducing, input_dim, output_dim; 
                     kernel_variance=1.0f0, noise_variance=0.1f0)
    # Initialize inducing points randomly
    inducing_points = randn(Float32, input_dim, num_inducing)
    
    # Initialize kernel parameters
    lengthscales = ones(Float32, input_dim)
    
    # Mean function (can be learned)
    mean_function = Dense(input_dim => output_dim)
    
    return DeepGPLayer(
        num_inducing, input_dim, output_dim,
        inducing_points, lengthscales, kernel_variance, noise_variance,
        mean_function
    )
end

Flux.@functor DeepGPLayer

# RBF kernel for GP
function rbf_kernel(X1, X2, lengthscales, variance)
    # X1: (input_dim, n1), X2: (input_dim, n2)  
    # Output: (n1, n2) kernel matrix
    
    n1, n2 = size(X1, 2), size(X2, 2)
    K = zeros(Float32, n1, n2)
    
    for i in 1:n1, j in 1:n2
        sq_dist = sum(((X1[:, i] - X2[:, j]) ./ lengthscales).^2)
        K[i, j] = variance * exp(-0.5f0 * sq_dist)
    end
    
    return K
end

# Variational inference for Deep GP
function (layer::DeepGPLayer)(X, training=true)
    batch_size = size(X, 2)
    
    # Compute kernel matrices
    K_uu = rbf_kernel(layer.inducing_points, layer.inducing_points, 
                      layer.kernel_lengthscales, layer.kernel_variance)
    K_uu += I * 1e-6  # Numerical stability
    
    K_uf = rbf_kernel(layer.inducing_points, X, 
                      layer.kernel_lengthscales, layer.kernel_variance)
    
    # Mean function contribution
    mean_pred = layer.mean_function(X')  # X is transposed for Dense layer
    
    if training
        # Return mean prediction during training (simplified)
        return mean_pred'
    else
        # Full posterior computation for prediction
        L_uu = cholesky(K_uu).L
        A = L_uu \ K_uf
        
        # Predictive mean (simplified - full implementation would use variational parameters)
        pred_mean = mean_pred' + A' * randn(Float32, layer.num_inducing, layer.output_dim)
        
        return pred_mean
    end
end

# Multi-layer Deep GP for spatio-temporal data
struct SpatioTemporalDeepGP
    layers::Vector{DeepGPLayer}
    num_layers::Int
end

function SpatioTemporalDeepGP(layer_dims, num_inducing_points)
    num_layers = length(layer_dims) - 1
    layers = DeepGPLayer[]
    
    for i in 1:num_layers
        push!(layers, DeepGPLayer(num_inducing_points, layer_dims[i], layer_dims[i+1]))
    end
    
    return SpatioTemporalDeepGP(layers, num_layers)
end

Flux.@functor SpatioTemporalDeepGP

function (dgp::SpatioTemporalDeepGP)(X, training=true)
    h = X
    for layer in dgp.layers
        h = layer(h, training)
    end
    return h
end

# Example: Spatio-temporal modeling with Deep GP
function deep_gp_spatiotemporal_example()
    # Define layer dimensions: [space-time coords, hidden1, hidden2, output]
    layer_dims = [3, 20, 10, 1]  # 2D space + 1D time -> scalar output
    num_inducing = 50
    
    model = SpatioTemporalDeepGP(layer_dims, num_inducing)
    
    # Generate synthetic spatio-temporal data
    n_points = 200
    
    # Random spatial coordinates
    spatial_coords = rand(Float32, 2, n_points) * 10  # 10x10 spatial domain
    temporal_coords = rand(Float32, 1, n_points) * 5  # Time from 0 to 5
    
    # Combine into spatio-temporal coordinates
    X = vcat(spatial_coords, temporal_coords)  # (3, n_points)
    
    # Forward pass
    predictions = model(X, false)  # Prediction mode
    
    println("Deep GP predictions shape: $(size(predictions))")
    println("Input shape: $(size(X))")
    
    return predictions, X
end

predictions, coords = deep_gp_spatiotemporal_example()

2.15 8.5 Neural Processes for Spatio-Temporal Data

Neural Processes (NPs) combine the flexibility of neural networks with the uncertainty quantification of Gaussian processes. They are particularly well-suited for spatio-temporal data where we want to make predictions at arbitrary space-time locations.

2.15.1 8.5.1 Conditional Neural Processes

Definition 8.7 (Conditional Neural Process). A Conditional Neural Process defines a stoch
